¶

Flow-Based Network Intrusion Detection — Machine Learning Study¶

Author: Luis Rito Filipe¶

June, 2023¶


Summary¶

This study evaluates multiple machine learning models for anomaly detection in network traffic, with the objective of maximizing the detection of network attacks while limiting false positives.

Among the evaluated models, LightGBM was selected as the final model, as it consistently achieved high performance across all evaluation metrics and satisfied the defined constraint (Recall ≥ 0.99). While differences between the top-performing models are minimal, LightGBM provides a favorable balance between recall and precision under threshold tuning, aligning with the objective of maximizing attack detection while maintaining acceptable false positive rates.

The results further indicate that attack detection is largely driven by a limited subset of flow-based features. Despite variations in feature importance across models, all approaches achieve near-perfect classification performance, suggesting that the dataset exhibits strong intrinsic separability in the selected feature space.

About The Study¶

The objective of this study is to compare machine learning algorithms when applied to data analysis to detect network attacks. To achieve this, I will leverage widely used Python libraries specialized in data analysis and machine learning, including tools for data manipulation, visualization, and predictive modeling. The study will progress from exploratory data analysis to more advanced techniques, such as feature engineering and fraud detection modeling, to provide meaningful insights into the characteristics of network attacks detection.

About The Dataset¶

The following study uses a dataset of network intrusion publicly available from Kaggle (see link below). The dataset was produced by Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity.

Dataset link: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/chethuhn/network-intrusion-dataset

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
#import time
#import threading
#import csv, sqlite3
#import statistics
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
from matplotlib.colors import LinearSegmentedColormap, TwoSlopeNorm, Normalize
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
#import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import seaborn as sns
#from datetime import date, timedelta
# from ibapi.client  import EClient
# from ibapi.wrapper import EWrapper
# from ibapi.contract import Contract
# from ibapi.ticktype import TickTypeEnum
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, precision_recall_curve, roc_auc_score, roc_curve, average_precision_score, matthews_corrcoef, log_loss, brier_score_loss
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV, StratifiedKFold
# from sklearn.svm import SVC
# from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
# from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from catboost import CatBoostClassifier
# from sklearn.neural_network import MLPClassifier
# from sklearn import metrics
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from lightgbm import LGBMClassifier
import shap
import warnings

pd.set_option('display.max_rows', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")


def plot_confusion_matrix(y, y_predict):
    cm = confusion_matrix(y, y_predict)
    ax = plt.subplot()
    cmap = "Reds"
    sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap=cmap, ax = ax)
    ax.set_xlabel('Predicted labels')
    ax.set_ylabel('True labels')
    ax.set_title('Confusion Matrix', pad=14, fontweight="bold")
    ax.xaxis.set_ticklabels(['Is not fraud', 'Is fraud'])
    ax.yaxis.set_ticklabels(['Is not fraud', 'Is fraud'])
    plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (4.2, 2.5)
    plt.show()

Data Wrangling¶

Two datasets with exact the same 79 features, one with DDoS attacks and other with PortScan attacks, were used. After loading and merging the datasets, data was prepared.

df2 = pd.read_csv("Friday-WorkingHours-Afternoon-PortScan.pcap_ISCX.csv")

In this study only a subset of features was initially used (Flow Bytes/s, Flow Packets/s, Fwd Packets/s, Bwd Packets/s, Subflow Fwd Packets, Subflow Fwd Bytes, Packet Length Std, Packet Length Variance, Flow Duration, Init_Win_bytes_forward, Init_Win_bytes_backward, Down/Up Ratio, Label).

There are 10 features with only one unique value, which imply they have zero variance and cannot help distinguishing Label 0 vs Label 1. Mathematically, for models like logistic regression, the coefficient becomes meaningless and it may even cause numerical instability in some cases. If any of these were included in the subset used, it would also be removed.

Label feature contains three unique values. "BENIGN" will be replaced by 0 (normal), and "DDoS" and "PortScan" by 1 (attack). Label feature was renamed to Class. A space before or after each feature name was removed. A few rows with infinite values were removed. Rows with values of -1 in features "Init_Win_bytes_forward" and "Init_Win_bytes_backward" were removed as this is an indication that the information was not available or not applicable for a particular flow.

In [6]:
df.columns = df.columns.str.strip()
df = df[['Flow Bytes/s', 'Flow Packets/s', 'Fwd Packets/s', 'Bwd Packets/s', 'Subflow Fwd Packets', 'Subflow Fwd Bytes', 'Packet Length Std', 'Packet Length Variance', 'Flow Duration', 'Init_Win_bytes_forward', 'Init_Win_bytes_backward', 'Down/Up Ratio', 'Label']]
remove_list = []
for i in range(len(df.columns)):
    if (len(df[df.columns[i]].unique()) == 1):
        remove_list.append(df.columns[i])
df["Label"] = df["Label"].isin(["DDoS", "PortScan"]).astype(int)
df = df.rename(columns={"Label": "Class"})
df.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan, inplace=True)
df = df.dropna().reset_index(drop=True)
df = df[(df["Init_Win_bytes_forward"] != -1) & (df["Init_Win_bytes_backward"] != -1)]

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Analyzing feature correlations helped identify strongly correlated feature pairs, allowing for the removal of redundant variables and improving model robustness. Below, the summary statistics of the dataset reduced and prepared for the machine learning algorithms. The dataset shows moderate class imbalance (69.3% attack traffic).


In [7]:
def correlation_check(X, threshold=0.95):
    corr_matrix = X.corr().abs()
    
    upper = corr_matrix.where(
        np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool)
    )

    to_drop = [
        column for column in upper.columns
        if any(upper[column] > threshold)
    ]

    return to_drop, corr_matrix

corr_check = correlation_check(df, threshold=0.95)[0]
df = df.drop(columns=corr_check)
df.describe()
Out[7]:

The following chart illustrates the correlation between the target variable Class and the independent features. The results indicate that no feature exhibits a strong correlation, positive or negative.

It is important to note that correlation measures linear relationships only and does not capture potential nonlinear interactions between variables. Therefore, features with low correlation may still contribute to model performance when combined with other variables or when nonlinear effects are considered.

In [8]:
def plot_feature_correlation():
    corr = df.corr()["Class"].drop("Class").sort_values(ascending=False)
    sns.set_style("whitegrid")
    
    full_cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
        "red_white_blue",
        ["#b2182b", "#ffffff", "#08306b"]
    )
    def get_colors(values):
        vmin, vmax = values.min(), values.max()

        if vmin < 0 and vmax > 0:
            norm = TwoSlopeNorm(vmin=vmin, vcenter=0, vmax=vmax)
            cmap = full_cmap
        elif vmin >= 0:
            norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
            cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
                "white_blue",
                ["#DEEBF7", "#08306B"]
            )
        else:
            norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
            cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
                "red_white",
                ["#B2182B", "#DEEBF7"]
            )
        return list(cmap(norm(values)))

    values = corr.values
    colors = get_colors(values)

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 3.2))

    sns.barplot(
        x=corr.index,
        y=corr.values,
        hue=corr.index,
        palette=colors,
        legend=False,
        ax=ax
    )

    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=90)
    ax.set_title("Correlation with Class", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", pad=14)
    ax.set_xlabel(None)
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    ax.axhline(y=0, color="black", linewidth=0.3)

    fig.canvas.draw()
    renderer = fig.canvas.get_renderer()
    max_height_px = max(
        label.get_window_extent(renderer=renderer).height
        for label in ax.get_xticklabels()
    )
    dpi = fig.dpi
    extra_height_inch = max_height_px / dpi
    fig.set_size_inches(12, 3 + extra_height_inch)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_feature_correlation()

Boxplot analysis highlights that attack traffic (Class 1) generally exhibits fewer outliers across most features, indicating more regular and constrained behavior compared to normal traffic, particularly in features such as “Subflow Fwd Bytes” and “Subflow Fwd Packets”. This supports the idea that normal traffic, associated with browsing, streaming, downloads, and API interactions, is more diverse, whereas attack traffic tends to be automated and repetitive in nature. However, feature "Flow Duration" shows notable outliers for attack traffic, suggesting occasional extreme deviations, possibly corresponding to bursts of activity or sustained attack flows. Overall, attack patterns appear more structured but also revealing sporadic high-intensity events.
In [9]:
def plot_box_subplots():
    corr = df.corr(numeric_only=True)["Class"].drop("Class").abs().sort_values(ascending=False)
    features = corr.index
    n_features = len(features)
    cols = 4
    rows = int(np.ceil(n_features / cols))
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(rows, cols, figsize=(18, rows * 2.5))

    axes = axes.flatten()
    df1 = df.copy()
    # for col in features:
    #     if (df1[col] >= 0).all():
    #         df1[col] = np.log1p(df1[col])

    for i, col in enumerate(features):
        df1.boxplot(column=col, by="Class", ax=axes[i], grid=False)
        axes[i].set_title(col, fontsize=13)
        axes[i].set_xlabel("")
        
    for j in range(len(features), len(axes)):
        fig.delaxes(axes[j])

    plt.suptitle("Feature Distribution by Class", y=1.001, fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_box_subplots()

In the next step, the dataset is split into training and testing sets to allow proper training of the classification models and unbiased evaluation of their performance.
In [10]:
models = dict()
X = df.drop("Class", axis=1)
Y = df["Class"]
X_train, X_test, Y_train, Y_test = train_test_split(X, Y, test_size=0.33, random_state=42, stratify=Y)

print('X_train.shape=', X_train.shape, 'Y_train.shape=', Y_train.shape)
print('X_test.shape=', X_test.shape, 'Y_test.shape=', Y_test.shape)

X_train.shape= (232251, 9) Y_train.shape= (232251,)
X_test.shape= (114393, 9) Y_test.shape= (114393,)

Logistic Regression

Logistic Regression is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for classification tasks, particularly binary classification problems where the target variable takes values such as 0/1, true/false, or yes/no. In this study, it is applied to predict the "Class" variable, which indicates whether a network flows are associated with attacks or not. Despite its simplicity, Logistic Regression can perform well in high-dimensional datasets and provide a strong baseline for comparison with more complex models. Given the large number of observations relative to the number of features in this dataset, the model is less likely to overfit, although regularization and class imbalance handling remain important considerations. Class weights are incorporated to address the strong imbalance between attacks and non-attacks. The coefficients of the Logistic Regression model provide insight into the direction and relative importance of each feature. Positive values indicate an increase in the likelihood of a network flow being classified as attack, while negative values indicate the opposite. The magnitude of the coefficients reflects the strength of the relationship. Below is the implementation of Logistic Regression with hyperparameter tuning, with emphasis on the adjustment of class weights.

In [11]:
def run_logistic_regression():
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
    X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)
    
    parameters = {
            "solver": ['lbfgs'],
            "class_weight": ["balanced"],
            "C": [0.01, 0.1, 1],
            "max_iter": [1000]        
        }

    lr = LogisticRegression()

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=lr,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="f1",
        cv=10,
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train_scaled, Y_train)
    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
    proba = cv.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)  # fixed threshold
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("log_regression_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("log_regression_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    coefficients = best_model.coef_[0]
    feature_names = X.columns
    df_coef = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": feature_names,
        "Coefficient": coefficients
    })
    df_coef = df_coef.sort_values("Coefficient", ascending=False).round(3).reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "coefficients": df_coef, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

log_regression = run_logistic_regression()
models.update({"Logistic Regression": log_regression})

Fitting 10 folds for each of 3 candidates, totalling 30 fits
In [12]:
log_regression["metrics"].iloc[50:55]

Threshold PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score TP TN FP FN MCC

52 0.52 0.936558 0.995093 0.964939 78890 29770 5344 389 0.882358
53 0.53 0.936656 0.995068 0.964979 78888 29779 5335 391 0.882497
54 0.54 0.937046 0.995068 0.965186 78888 29814 5300 391 0.883211

Out[13]:
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Packet Length Std Flow Duration Init_Win_bytes_forward Flow Packets/s Flow Bytes/s Init_Win_bytes_backward Down/Up Ratio Subflow Fwd Bytes Subflow Fwd Packets
Coefficient 10.632 1.421 0.995 0.073 -0.303 -1.012 -1.733 -42.989 -46.788

A confusion matrix is shown below for a minimum probability equal to the threshold corresponding to the maximum F1Score for a RecallScore equal or higher than 0.8.
In [34]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, log_regression["y_proba"] >= log_regression["metrics"][log_regression["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0])

Random Forest

Random Forest is often expected to outperform Logistic Regression in network intrusion tasks, as it can capture nonlinear patterns, interactions between features, and is generally more robust regarding noise. The Random Forest hyperparameter search was optimized to reduce computational cost/time by limiting tree complexity and reducing cross-validation folds, while preserving model comparability across experiments.

In [15]:
def run_random_forest():
    parameters = {
            "class_weight": ["balanced"],
            "n_estimators": [200],
            "max_depth": [10],
            "min_samples_leaf": [5],
            "max_features": ["sqrt"]
        }

    rf = RandomForestClassifier( random_state=42, n_jobs=-1 )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=rf,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=6,             # random combinations
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="recall",
        cv=StratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42),                  # cross-validation, 3 or StratifiedKFold(n_splits=2)
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("random_forest_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("random_forest_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    importances = best_model.feature_importances_
    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns,
        "Importance": importances
    })
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round(3).reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

random_forest = run_random_forest()
models.update({"Random Forest": random_forest})

Fitting 3 folds for each of 1 candidates, totalling 3 fits
In [16]:
random_forest["metrics"].iloc[50:55]
Out[16]:
Threshold PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score TP TN FP FN MCC
50 0.50 0.999962 0.999836 0.999899 79266 35111 3 13 0.999671
51 0.51 0.999962 0.999836 0.999899 79266 35111 3 13 0.999671
52 0.52 0.999975 0.999836 0.999905 79266 35112 2 13 0.999692
53 0.53 0.999987 0.999811 0.999899 79264 35113 1 15 0.999671
54 0.54 0.999987 0.999798 0.999893 79263 35113 1 16 0.999651

The following table shows the computed feature importance ordered from the highest to the lowest.
In [17]:
random_forest["importances"]
Out[17]:
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Bytes Flow Bytes/s Init_Win_bytes_backward Packet Length Std Init_Win_bytes_forward Flow Packets/s Flow Duration Subflow Fwd Packets Down/Up Ratio
Importance 0.235 0.207 0.19 0.147 0.069 0.051 0.037 0.034 0.03

There is a noticeable difference in the importance attributed to features "Subflow Fwd Bytes" by each algorithm. "Subflow Fwd Bytes" measures how much forward data is transmitted within segments of a flow, providing insight into how traffic volume is distributed rather than just the total amount. A subflow is a segment of a larger flow. Attack flows (Class 1) tend to send less data forward per segment. This feature may reveal important patterns that can be captured by tree models such as Random Forest.

A confusion matrix is shown below for a minimum probability equal to the threshold corresponding to the maximum F1Score for a RecallScore equal or higher than 0.8.

In [18]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, random_forest["y_proba"] >= random_forest["metrics"][random_forest["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0])

XGBoost

XGBoost is expected to show similar metrics compared to Random Forest in this task. Reducing the subsample and colsample_bytree (min ≥ 0.7) from default values (1.0) introduces randomness, reduces overfitting, improves generalization, forces feature diversity and reduces dominance of strong features. Shares some similarities with Random Forest. But while the later builds trees during training and outputs the average of the predictions from individual trees, XGBoost builds new trees sequentially and each new tree corrects errors from the previous tree, improving model's predictions.

In [19]:
def run_xgboost():
    # Note: learning_rate -> estabilidade [0.02-0.05], max_depth -> complexidade [6-12], scale_pos_weight é o parâmetro mais importante para um recall elevado a probabilidades mais elevadas (thresholds)
    scale_pos_weight = len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(0)]) / len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(1)])

    parameters = {
        "n_estimators": [200, 300, 400],
        "max_depth": [8, 9, 10, 11, 12],
        "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.02, 0.05],
        "subsample": [0.7, 0.8, 1.0],
        "colsample_bytree": [0.7, 0.8, 1.0],
        "scale_pos_weight": [scale_pos_weight*0.5, scale_pos_weight, scale_pos_weight*1.5]
    }

    xgb = XGBClassifier( random_state=42, n_jobs=-1, eval_metric="logloss" )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=xgb,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=20,             # 20 random combinations
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="recall",
        cv=StratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42),
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("xgboost_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("xgboost_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    importances = best_model.get_booster().get_score(importance_type="gain")
    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns
    })
    df_importances["Importance"] = df_importances["Feature"].map(importances).fillna(0)
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round().reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

xgboost = run_xgboost()
models.update({"XGBoost": xgboost})

Fitting 3 folds for each of 20 candidates, totalling 60 fits
In [20]:
xgboost["metrics"].iloc[50:55]
Out[20]:

51 0.51 0.999924 0.999937 0.999931 79274 35108 6 5 0.999774
52 0.52 0.999924 0.999937 0.999931 79274 35108 6 5 0.999774
53 0.53 0.999924 0.999937 0.999931 79274 35108 6 5 0.999774
54 0.54 0.999924 0.999924 0.999924 79273 35108 6 6 0.999753

The following table shows the computed feature importance from the highest to the lowest.

xgboost["importances"]
Out[21]:
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Flow Bytes/s Subflow Fwd Bytes Packet Length Std Init_Win_bytes_backward Subflow Fwd Packets Init_Win_bytes_forward Flow Packets/s Down/Up Ratio Flow Duration
Importance 1092.0 817.0 799.0 349.0 138.0 125.0 67.0 67.0 13.0

A confusion matrix is shown below for a minimum probability equal to the threshold corresponding to the maximum F1Score for a RecallScore equal or higher than 0.8.
In [22]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, xgboost["y_proba"] >= xgboost["metrics"][xgboost["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0])

LightGBM

LightGBM is a high-performance gradient boosting framework built on decision tree algorithms. It is widely used for ranking, classification, and many other machine learning tasks. Works particularly well with large datasets and a large number of independent variables, optimizing heavily for speed and memory efficiency. LightGBM grows trees leaf-wise, evaluates all leaves and expands the single leaf that reduces the loss the most. It uses histogram-based algorithms to group continuous feature values into discrete bins. This drastically reduces memory usage and speeds up training. It can handle categorical features directly without requiring to manually apply encoding first. Because leaf-wise growth is quite aggressive, LightGBM can easily overfit, especially on smaller datasets. To prevent this, careful tuning of hyperparameters like max_depth, num_leaves, and min_data_in_leaf is strictly required.

In [23]:
def run_light_gbm():
    scale_pos_weight = len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(0)]) / len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(1)])

    parameters = {
        "n_estimators": [200, 300, 400],
        "max_depth": [-1], # -1 = sem restrições
        "num_leaves": [31, 63, 127],
        "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.02, 0.05],
        "scale_pos_weight": [scale_pos_weight*0.5, scale_pos_weight, scale_pos_weight*1.5]
    }

    lgbm = LGBMClassifier( random_state=42, n_jobs=-1 )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=lgbm,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=20,             # 20 random combinations
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="f1",
        cv=StratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42),
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("light_gbm_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("light_gbm_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns,
        "Importance": best_model.booster_.feature_importance(importance_type="gain")
    })
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round().reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

light_gbm = run_light_gbm()
models.update({"LightGBM": light_gbm})

Fitting 3 folds for each of 20 candidates, totalling 60 fits

0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Flow Bytes/s Init_Win_bytes_backward Subflow Fwd Bytes Packet Length Std Init_Win_bytes_forward Flow Packets/s Subflow Fwd Packets Down/Up Ratio Flow Duration
Importance 698680.0 510218.0 501723.0 182248.0 41158.0 5448.0 4223.0 3728.0 1305.0

A confusion matrix is shown below for a minimum probability equal to the threshold corresponding to the maximum F1Score for a RecallScore equal or higher than 0.8.
In [26]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, light_gbm["y_proba"] >= light_gbm["metrics"][light_gbm["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0])

CatBoost

CatBoost is a high-performance gradient boosting framework built on decision tree algorithms. It is widely used for classification, regression, and ranking tasks, particularly on tabular data. CatBoost introduces ordered boosting, a technique designed to reduce overfitting by preventing information leakage during training. It also uses symmetric trees, where the same splitting condition is applied across each level, improving prediction speed and model stability. One of its main advantages is the ability to handle categorical features directly without requiring extensive preprocessing. CatBoost is generally robust to hyperparameter choices and requires less tuning compared to other gradient boosting frameworks. However, it can be slower to train than LightGBM, especially on very large datasets.

Although CatBoost provides a large number of configurable hyperparameters, it typically requires less manual tuning than frameworks such as LightGBM or XGBoost, due to its robust default settings and built-in mechanisms for handling categorical features and reducing overfitting.

In [27]:
def run_cat_boost():

    parameters = {
        "auto_class_weights": ["Balanced"],
        "iterations": [300],
        "depth": [4, 6, 8],
        "learning_rate": [0.02, 0.05, 0.1],
        "loss_function": ['Logloss'],
        "eval_metric": ["F1"],
        "verbose": [0]
    }

    catboost = CatBoostClassifier( random_state=42, thread_count=-1 )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=catboost,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=10,
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="f1",
        cv=StratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42),
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("cat_boost_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("cat_boost_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns,
        "Importance": best_model.get_feature_importance()
    })
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round().reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

cat_boost = run_cat_boost()
models.update({"CatBoost": cat_boost})

Fitting 3 folds for each of 9 candidates, totalling 27 fits
In [28]:
cat_boost["metrics"].iloc[50:55]
Out[28]:
Threshold PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score TP TN FP FN MCC
50 0.50 0.999937 0.999937 0.999937 79274 35109 5 5 0.999795
51 0.51 0.999937 0.999937 0.999937 79274 35109 5 5 0.999795
52 0.52 0.999950 0.999937 0.999943 79274 35110 4 5 0.999815
53 0.53 0.999950 0.999937 0.999943 79274 35110 4 5 0.999815
54 0.54 0.999950 0.999937 0.999943 79274 35110 4 5 0.999815

The following table shows the computed feature importance from the highest to the lowest.
In [29]:
cat_boost["importances"]
Out[29]:
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Bytes Init_Win_bytes_backward Packet Length Std Init_Win_bytes_forward Down/Up Ratio Flow Bytes/s Flow Duration Flow Packets/s Subflow Fwd Packets
Importance 35.0 25.0 13.0 9.0 6.0 5.0 3.0 3.0 2.0

A confusion matrix is shown below for a minimum probability equal to the threshold corresponding to the maximum F1Score for a RecallScore equal or higher than 0.8.
In [30]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, cat_boost["y_proba"] >= cat_boost["metrics"][cat_boost["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0] )

Model Comparison and Selection

The next step is to define the desired model outcome. If minimizing false positives is the priority in attacks detection, then configurations with higher precision should be preferred. However, this comes at the cost of increased false negatives, meaning that more attacks may go undetected.

Model selection is performed by first enforcing a minimum Recall of 0.99, ensuring that a sufficient proportion of attacks are detected. Among the models satisfying this constraint, the one with the best metrics overall and calibration curve is selected, as it provides the best balance between precision and recall. This approach aligns model performance with the business requirements of maximizing attacks detection while controlling the number of false positives. This selection process reflects a practical decision framework, where recall acts as a hard requirement and the remaining metrics are used for performance optimization within that constraint.

In [47]:
def model_comparison():
    df_all = []
    
    for name, model in models.items():
        df = model["metrics"].copy()
        df["Model"] = name
        cols = ["Threshold", "PrecisionScore", "RecallScore", "F1Score", "TP", "TN", "FP", "FN", "MCC"]
        df = df[["Model"] + [c for c in cols if c in df.columns]]
        logloss = log_loss(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        brier = brier_score_loss(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        roc = roc_auc_score(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        pr = average_precision_score(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        df["LogLoss"] = logloss
        df["BrierScoreLoss"] = brier
        df["ROC_AUC"] = roc
        df["PR_AUC"] = pr
        df_all.append(df)
    
    df_all = pd.concat(df_all).reset_index(drop=True)

    models_best_performance = df_all[df_all["RecallScore"] >= 0.99].sort_values(["F1Score"], ascending=[False]).drop_duplicates("Model").reset_index(drop=True)

    return { "models_best_performance": models_best_performance, "comparison_across_threshold": df_all }

df_comparison = model_comparison()
In [48]:
df_comparison["models_best_performance"]
Out[48]:
Model Threshold PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score TP TN FP FN MCC LogLoss BrierScoreLoss ROC_AUC PR_AUC
0 LightGBM 0.28 0.999937 0.999975 0.999956 79277 35109 5 2 0.999856 0.000623 0.000067 0.999985 0.999995
1 XGBoost 0.79 0.999975 0.999924 0.999950 79273 35112 2 6 0.999836 0.000469 0.000074 0.999988 0.999996
2 CatBoost 0.52 0.999950 0.999937 0.999943 79274 35110 4 5 0.999815 0.000526 0.000074 0.999989 0.999996
3 Random Forest 0.38 0.999962 0.999886 0.999924 79270 35111 3 9 0.999753 0.000984 0.000137 0.999983 0.999994
4 Logistic Regression 0.69 0.989481 0.991927 0.990703 78639 34278 836 640 0.969635 0.170828 0.047918 0.991434 0.994889

Based on the model comparison results, LightGBM, XGBoost, CatBoost, and Random Forest exhibit very similar performance, with only marginal differences across evaluation metrics.

All four tree-based models achieve near-perfect recall and precision, indicating that the dataset is highly separable using the selected flow-based features. LightGBM achieves slightly higher recall and F1-score at a tuned decision threshold while maintaining a very low number of false positives, which is desirable in intrusion detection systems where missed attacks are costly.

Although LightGBM shows marginally lower ROC-AUC and PR-AUC values compared to other models, these differences are negligible and do not impact practical performance.

Logistic Regression also performs strongly, achieving high precision and recall, which suggests that the underlying patterns in the data are largely linearly separable. However, ensemble tree-based methods provide slight improvements by capturing non-linear interactions between features.

Overall, the extremely high performance across all models indicates that the dataset exhibits strong intrinsic separability, rather than the superiority of a specific algorithm.

In the context of network intrusion detection, recall is the most critical metric, as failing to detect attacks has severe consequences. Precision remains important to limit false positives and maintain system usability. Given these considerations, and its slightly superior recall performance under threshold tuning, LightGBM is selected as the final model.

It is important to note that the dataset presents moderate class imbalance (aprox. 69% attack traffic), and the near-perfect evaluation metrics suggest that results may be influenced by the structured nature of the dataset.

In [49]:
def plot_model_comparison():

    fig = plt.figure(figsize=(16, 5))
    fig.suptitle( "ML Model Performance at Maximum F1Score for Recall ≥ 0.99  |  Precision & Recall vs. Threshold", y=0.98, fontsize=16, fontweight="bold" )
    gs = gridspec.GridSpec(2, 3, width_ratios=[2, 1, 1])

    df_comparison_filtered = df_comparison["comparison_across_threshold"][df_comparison["comparison_across_threshold"]["Model"].isin(list(df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].head(4)))]
    data = df_comparison_filtered[~df_comparison_filtered["Threshold"].isin([0.0, 1.0])]

    df_melt = df_comparison["models_best_performance"].head(4).melt(
        id_vars="Model",
        value_vars=["PrecisionScore", "RecallScore", "F1Score"]
    )

    ax_main = fig.add_subplot(gs[:, 0])

    sns.barplot(
        data=df_melt,
        x="Model",
        y="value",
        hue="variable",
        ax=ax_main,
        palette="Reds" # "Blues", "Reds", "Oranges", "Greens", "Purples", "Greys", "Blues", "deep", "muted", "dark", "colorblind",
    )

    ax_main.set_ylabel("Score", fontsize=12)
    ax_main.set_ylim(0.9995, 1)
    ax_main.set_xlabel("")
    ax_main.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=0, pad=10)
    ax_main.legend(loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.12), ncol=3)

    ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 1])
    ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 2], sharey=ax1)
    ax3 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1], sharex=ax1)
    ax4 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 2], sharex=ax2, sharey=ax3)
    axes_right = [ax1, ax2, ax3, ax4]

    for ax in [ax1, ax2]:
        ax.tick_params(labelbottom=False)
    for ax in [ax2, ax4]:
        ax.tick_params(labelleft=False)

    models = data["Model"].unique()

    for ax, model in zip(axes_right, models):

        data_model = data[data["Model"] == model]

        df_long = data_model.melt(
            id_vars="Threshold",
            value_vars=["PrecisionScore", "RecallScore"],
            var_name="Metric",
            value_name="Score"
        )

        sns.lineplot(
            data=df_long,
            x="Threshold",
            y="Score",
            hue="Metric",
            ax=ax,
            palette="Set1",
            linewidth=2
        )

        ax.set_title(model, fontsize=13, pad=10)
        ax.set_ylim(0.999, 1.0002)
        ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
        ax.set_xlabel("Threshold", fontsize=12)
        ax.set_ylabel("Score", fontsize=12)
        ax.legend().remove()

    handles, _ = axes_right[0].get_legend_handles_labels()

    fig.legend(
        handles,
        ["Precision", "Recall"],
        loc="lower center",
        bbox_to_anchor=(0.75, 0.02),
        ncol=2,
        frameon=True
    )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_model_comparison()

The ROC curves for the top four models are nearly identical and exhibit a near-square shape, indicating extremely high class separability and similar ranking performance. This suggests that model choice has minimal impact, as the underlying feature space already allows for near-perfect discrimination between classes. Differences in classification metrics are therefore negligible and within expected variance.
In [50]:
def model_roc_curve():

    valid_models = set(df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].head(4))
    filtered_models = {k: v for k, v in models.items() if k in valid_models}
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4))

    for i, (name, model) in enumerate(filtered_models.items()):
        fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        auc = roc_auc_score(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        df = pd.DataFrame({"false_positive_rate": fpr, "true_positive_rate": tpr})
        sns.lineplot(
            data=df,
            x="false_positive_rate",
            y="true_positive_rate",
            ax=axes[i]            
            #label=name
        )
        axes[i].fill_between( df["false_positive_rate"], df["true_positive_rate"], alpha=0.3 )
        axes[i].set_title(f"{name} (AUC = {auc:.3f})", fontsize=14, pad=14)
        axes[i].set_xlabel("False Positive Rate", fontsize=12)
        axes[i].set_ylabel("True Positive Rate", fontsize=12)

    plt.suptitle("ROC_AUC Curve (Top 4 Models)", y=0.98, fontsize=16, fontweight="bold")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

model_roc_curve()

The calibration curves for the top-performing models exhibit irregular shapes, particularly in the mid-probability range. This behavior is primarily due to the highly confident predictions produced by the models, with most probabilities concentrated near 0 or 1. As a result, intermediate probability bins contain relatively few samples, leading to instability in the estimated true probabilities. This reflects the strong separability of the dataset rather than poor model performance. While tree-based models tend to produce overconfident probability estimates, the limited presence of ambiguous cases reduces the practical importance of calibration in this context.
In [51]:
def model_calibration_curve():

    valid_models = set(df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].head(4))
    filtered_models = {k: v for k, v in models.items() if k in valid_models}
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4.5))

    for i, (name, model) in enumerate(filtered_models.items()):
        bins = 11
        prob_true, prob_pred = calibration_curve(Y_test, model["y_proba"], n_bins=bins)
        df = pd.DataFrame({"prob_true": prob_true, "prob_pred": prob_pred})
        
        # modelo
        sns.lineplot(
            data=df,
            x="prob_pred",
            y="prob_true",
            ax=axes[i],
            marker='o',
            label="Model"
        )

        # Perfeitamente calibrada
        sns.lineplot(
            x=[0, 1],
            y=[0, 1],
            ax=axes[i],
            linestyle='--',
            label="Perfectly calibrated"
        )
        
        axes[i].set_title(f"{name}", fontsize=14, pad=14)
        axes[i].set_xlabel("Predicted probability", fontsize=12)
        axes[i].set_ylabel("True probability", fontsize=12)
        axes[i].legend(loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.25), ncol=4)
    
    plt.suptitle("Calibration Curve (Top 4 Models)", y=0.98, fontsize=16, fontweight="bold")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

model_calibration_curve()

The plot indicates that attack detection is driven by several key features. Subflow Fwd Bytes shows the strongest influence, with higher values strongly associated with an increased probability of attack, while lower values have a less consistent effect. Higher values of Packet Length Std, Flow Bytes/s, and Init_Win_bytes_forward are also strongly associated with an increased likelihood of attacks. In contrast, Init_Win_bytes_backward and Down/Up Ratio contribute more moderately to the probability of attack classification.
In [ ]:
def features_impact(model):
    warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message="LightGBM binary classifier with TreeExplainer shap values output has changed")
    explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
    shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)
    return shap_values

def plot_summary_plot(model):
    shap_values = features_impact(model)
    shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_test, plot_size=(6, 8), show=False)
    ax = plt.gca()
    ax.tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=11)
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelsize=11)
    plt.xlabel("SHAP value (impact on model output)", fontsize=13, labelpad=15)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_summary_plot(models[df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].iloc[0]]['best_model'])

The SHAP dependence plot illustrates how Subflow Fwd Bytes influences model predictions, with values colored by Packet Length Std, the feature with the strongest interaction. The SHAP values are predominantly negative—mostly below −10—indicating that higher values of Subflow Fwd Bytes generally push predictions toward the non-attack class. At low values of Subflow Fwd Bytes, the contribution is more variable, reflecting greater model uncertainty. As the feature value increases, the SHAP values stabilize in the negative range. The interaction with Packet Length Std primarily affects the magnitude of this contribution: higher values of Packet Length Std tend to amplify the negative impact, while lower values are associated with more dispersed effects.
In [46]:
shap.dependence_plot("Subflow Fwd Bytes", features_impact(models[df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].iloc[0]]['best_model']), X_test)

Conclusion

Feature selection analysis, combined with correlation analysis, indicates that a reduced subset of variables captures most of the predictive signal, while several features are redundant, contribute negligibly, or introduce noise that can degrade model performance. The dataset contains features that provide a strong and clear signal for distinguishing attack traffic from normal traffic, resulting in near-perfect class separation across multiple models. This suggests that the high performance observed is driven not only by model capability, but also by the strong intrinsic separability of the dataset in the selected feature space.
